summary:
Caesar's Civil War (49–45 BC) was the four-year conflict between Julius Caesar and the forces of the Roman Senate and his former partner Pompey, fought across the Mediterranean world from Spain to Greece to Egypt and Africa, that ended in Caesar's total victory and the effective end of the Roman Republic as a functioning political system. The war was the culmination of decades of political polarization and the breakdown of republican norms documented through the [[event:1068|Gracchi Brothers Reforms]], [[event:1071|Sulla's Dictatorship]], and the [[event:1073|First Triumvirate]].

After [[event:1076|Crossing of the Rubicon]], Caesar swept through Italy so rapidly that Pompey and the Senate fled to Greece before they could organize resistance. Caesar then rapidly secured Spain — striking his enemies in the west before confronting Pompey in the east — before crossing to Greece. The decisive engagement came at the [[event:1078|Battle of Pharsalus]] in Thessaly in 48 BC, where Caesar's veterans defeated Pompey's larger but less battle-hardened army. Pompey fled to Egypt, where he was assassinated on the orders of the young Ptolemy XIII, who hoped to curry favor with Caesar.

Caesar spent much of 48–47 BC in Egypt, supporting [[event:1048|Cleopatra VII]] against her brother Ptolemy XIII — a winter that produced their son Caesarion and allowed Caesar's enemies to regroup in Africa and Spain. After victories in North Africa (Thapsus, 46 BC) and Spain (Munda, 45 BC), Caesar returned to Rome in triumph, holding unprecedented power with the title of perpetual dictator. His reforms were broadly beneficial — a new calendar (the Julian calendar, correcting centuries of accumulated error), debt relief, grain distributions, new colonies — but his monarchy in all but name alienated those who valued the Republic's forms.

The war's ultimate consequence was demonstrating that Roman political conflicts could only be resolved by military force — and that the winner could take everything. This lesson was not lost on the men who would continue fighting after Caesar's [[event:1079|Assassination of Julius Caesar]] in 44 BC.
